items.rationale: He sees stablecoins as crypto's proven product-market fit and the on-ramp to bring billions onchain, now that L2 transfer costs dropped below a penny; cites real-world inflation hedging (Argentina) and USD dominance.

citations.quote: Stablecoins are the "killer app" of crypto, and here's a bunch of great data that shows it.
Stablecoin payments and AI agents are some of the clearest use cases for crypto since Bitcoin's store of value.
In countries with rampant inflation, people are using stablecoins to protect their assets. Argentina is a prime example.

items.rationale: Most recent and rising theme: he believes AI agents could become crypto's primary users, dissolving UX friction, and that crypto democratizes AI by decentralizing compute/data while adding transparency.
items.age_note: Theme present since 2023-12-06 (AI democratization) but sharpened toward 'AI agents as users' in Dec 2024; weight the recent framing.

citations.quote: if AI agents become the primary users of crypto, a lot of the UX challenges (in the ways we have typically thought about them) go away.
Stablecoin payments and AI agents are some of the clearest use cases for crypto since Bitcoin's store of value.

items.rationale: His core 2025 thesis from State of Crypto research: only 5-10% of ~617M owners transact, so the biggest growth lever is converting dormant holders, not minting new investors.

citations.quote: I believe this is the year to re-engage the 617M "passive" crypto holders and bring the world onchain.
Let's close the gap between crypto owners and active on-chain users. This is more important... than expanding the total number of crypto investors.

items.rationale: Calls it an underrated category that will be critical to onboarding a billion users by importing real-world exogenous incentives (e.g., ESPN resolving prediction markets) into protocols.

citations.quote: zkTLS / zkEmail is an underrated category in crypto. Here are some intriguing real world use cases
I'm now convinced that web proofs/zkTLS will play a critical role in getting us there.

items.rationale: Views new SNARK designs as a new paradigm and a path to the endgame solution for blockchain scalability; consistently celebrates open-source proving breakthroughs.

citations.quote: Jolt is an insane accomplishment... a significant contribution toward the endgame solution for blockchain scalability, and of course the implementation is fully open source
Lasso and Jolt represent a new paradigm in SNARK design.

items.rationale: Anticipates >1,000x growth in online content from AI and argues we lack the infrastructure (provenance + incentivized curation) to handle it; explicitly excited about Story.

citations.quote: We desperately need an open, global coordination layer for IP, and I couldn't be more excited about what Story is building!
We need an open, global coordination layer in order for AI generated content to thrive. And this is exactly what blockchains can provide.

items.rationale: Sees them as a powerful real-time information / truth-surfacing tool; repeatedly argues US participation would make them more accurate, and notes odds shifted once Americans could legally participate.

citations.quote: Prediction markets have proven to be a powerful tool for surfacing real time information.
the odds notably shifted right after U.S. people... were allowed to legally participate.

items.rationale: Sees vibrant developer ecosystems forming around open social protocols, enabling user-owned identity/data and exit from extractive platforms.

citations.quote: The Decentralized Social experiment is underway, and vibrant developer ecosystems are emerging around open protocols like Farcaster.

items.rationale: Frames DAOs as an underappreciated funding source whose liquid treasuries exceed annual crypto VC, and is excited about expanded legal operating ability.

citations.quote: DAOs are an underappreciated source of funding for builders. DAO treasuries hold more in liquid tokens than the entire amount of VC investments into crypto companies last year.

items.rationale: Believes coordinating idle physical resources (GPUs, storage, telecom) via blockchains unlocks supply and democratizes access; lists it among promising early app experiments.

citations.quote: we're also seeing promising early experiments in areas like gaming, social, and DePIN.

items.rationale: Early-stage enthusiasm; growing share of new companies and he wants more builders solving hard scientific problems with blockchains.

citations.quote: It's early days for DeSci, but I'm grateful for entrepreneurs... using blockchains to solve some of the hardest - and most important - problems
The share of DeSci companies actually grew 20% from 2023 -> 2024!... would love to see more builders enter this space!

items.rationale: Persistent worry that the US is losing its web3 lead and may depend on untrusted foreign developers; treats regulatory clarity as both an industry need and national security matter.
items.age_note: Stated repeatedly 2023-2024; tone shifted to cautious optimism in late 2024 given 'political tailwinds,' but the underlying concern persists.

citations.quote: Keeping crypto onshore is a matter of national security. Will the U.S. learn from past experiences like semiconductor manufacturing?
The crypto industry has a real chance to get the regulatory clarity we deserve.

items.rationale: Worried that AI power is concentrating in a few firms with a black box; positions decentralized crypto networks as possibly the only credible counterbalancing force.

citations.quote: This consolidation is worrisome. Crypto might be the only credible counterbalancing force out there.
Decentralized blockchains are a counterbalancing force to centralized AI.

items.rationale: Sees a coming flood of AI content and deepfakes that needs proof-of-authenticity plus decentralized curation; black-box algorithms from a few companies 'won't cut it.'

citations.quote: proof of authenticity protocol and/or a decentralized network of incentivized curators feels like a big opportunity. Black box algorithms... won't cut it.
it also creates a world of abundant AI-generated content, including deep fakes.

items.rationale: Concerned that doubling wholesale CBDC development threatens the dollar's reserve status and US power; argues USD stablecoins are a better answer than a US CBDC.

citations.quote: wholesale CBDC developments have DOUBLED. This is a national security concern, since the dollar's status as a reserve currency gives the United States tremendous power.

items.rationale: Only an estimated 30-60M of ~617M owners transact monthly; active-address metrics are increasingly gamed by bots/incentives, making real adoption hard to measure and conversion the key challenge.
items.age_note: Framed as both a concern and his biggest opportunity; most-recent data work (Nov 2024) emphasizes how inflated raw address counts are.

citations.quote: Only 5-10% of crypto owners are transacting users.
Active addresses have lately become a really bad measure for users (low fees + incentives to game).

items.rationale: Raises (as an open debate) the risk that embedding wallets too deeply into the app stack abstracts blockchains away in a manner misaligned with web3's vision; lower-conviction, posed as a question.
items.age_note: Stated once (Jul 2024) as a debate prompt, not a firm position; never restated — persisted.

citations.quote: There is a real risk of over-abstracting wallets and blockchains from users. Embedding wallets too deeply into the application stack is misaligned with the vision of web3.

items.rationale: Held up as proof stablecoins work at scale now that L2 transfer costs fell below a penny — central to his bring-the-world-onchain thesis.

citations.quote: USDC transfers on Ethereum L2s now cost less than a penny. We're finally seeing the result of many years of hard work to scale blockchain infrastructure.

items.rationale: Repeatedly highlighted in his data as the leading chain by active addresses and DEX volume, driving recent activity spikes — observed positively, though framed as a metrics observation, not an investment call.

citations.quote: In March there were 37M active addresses across all tracked blockchains... The top chain by monthly active addresses: @solana.
Solana driving most of the recent spike.

items.rationale: Cited among low-fee chains capturing the majority of record DEX volume; consistent with his thesis that cheap transactions unlock consumer apps.

citations.quote: low-transaction fee blockchains like @base and @solana captured a majority of this activity.

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally bullish and builder-oriented: cites all-time-high adoption metrics, sub-penny L2 fees, strong political tailwinds, and the recurring price-innovation cycle, urging builders to 'trust the process.' His optimism is grounded in non-financial adoption data rather than price speculation (which he explicitly treats as a byproduct), giving a constructive, long-horizon risk-on posture.

notes: Matsuoka is a16z crypto's data/research voice, so his views read as adoption-thesis convictions more than tradeable token calls — he rarely endorses specific tokens and explicitly separates speculation from crypto's core value (2024-03-21). Highest-conviction, most-recent (Dec 2024) themes are stablecoin payments and AI agents as crypto's clearest use cases, plus a 2025 push to convert ~617M passive holders into active users. Crypto-x-AI is the dominant rising theme and cuts both ways: excited (democratizing AI, agents-as-users, IP coordination) and concerned (centralization, deepfakes). No genuine reversals found; older infra/regulatory views persist consistently. Tokens listed are inferred from his adoption data, not explicit buy theses — conviction scores reflect that hedge.
